text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down omental adhesions from the inflamed gallbladder, revealing its swollen and reddened appearance.

The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum from the gallbladder, ensuring that the transverse colon is not injured during the process. The transverse colon is visually checked to prevent it from being stuck to the omentum.
The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum, which is adherent to the inflamed gallbladder, using the cut mode to separate the structures without causing damage.
The surgeon switches to firefly mode, revealing the common bile duct with enhanced fluorescence. The intensity of the firefly imaging is adjusted, altering the visual background.

The surgeon exchanges instruments, moving the fundus retraction to port number four. The critical view of safety is achieved, and the surgeon clears fiber fatty tissue from the hepatocystic triangle, exposing the common bile duct to assist with trans-cystic CBD exploration.

A surgical clip is placed just below the infundibulum of the gallbladder. The surgeon milks the cystic duct towards the gallbladder before incising it. A small bleeding occurs, which is promptly controlled.

The surgeon inserts a five French cholangiocatheter through an introducer, using an eighteen gauge angiocath to facilitate placement. Wires are placed through the cholangiocatheter to stiffen it for easier passage through the cystic duct. The surgical robot's arm one is undocked to allow the C-arm to approach from the patient's right side.

The cholangi catheter is inserted and secured with the pro grasp in arm four. Arm two is undocked to position the C-arm optimally. Contrast is injected, revealing filling defects in the common bile duct, with visible flow of contrast into the duodenum. The proximity of the cystic duct to the common bile duct is noted, indicating the site of obstruction.

The surgeon upgrades from the cholangiogram catheter to a Cook common bile duct exploration set, inserting a dilator into the sheath. After removing the dilator, the sheath remains in place, and a balloon is inserted into the cystic duct. The balloon encounters difficulty passing due to a stone positioned adjacent to the common bile duct.

The surgeon makes another attempt to pass the dilator through the cystic duct and successfully inflates the balloon. The balloon is kept inflated for three minutes to dilate the cystic duct to approximately eight millimeters, facilitating the passage of the choledochoscope.

After three minutes of dilation, the balloon is deflated, and the colodoco scope is inserted into the cystic duct. The scope examines the proximal part, advancing deeper into the right hepatic branch, revealing the bifurcation with the left hepatic branch visible.

Blood is visible from the insertion site as the colodocoscope is passed distally. A stone is observed in the common bile duct. A four French four gritty balloon is used to dislodge the stone distally, allowing it to float within the duct.

The surgeon pulls to elevate the duodenum, performing multiple passes to clear fat from the top of the common bile duct. Retraction is achieved by lifting the falciform ligament, and the cystic duct is clipped.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy and places the gallbladder into a retrieval bag, leaving the bag in situ. The stone in the common bile duct is visible, which was not shown earlier.
The surgeon carefully dissects the fatty tissue from the top of the common bile duct, revealing the hepatic artery situated to the left and slightly deeper than the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses the cut mode to incise the common bile duct, revealing a stone that comes into view. Irrigation is applied to enhance visualization and assist in floating the stones for removal.

The surgeon extends the incision in the common bile duct to accommodate larger stones. A small bleeder is controlled before coagulation. Seven stones are removed from the common bile duct, with irrigation aiding in floating the stones for easier removal.

Another stone emerges from the distal common bile duct with irrigation. The surgeon milks the distal portion of the common bile duct to extract the stone, ensuring all floating stones are removed and placed in a bag for organization.

The surgeon continues to irrigate the common bile duct, milking the distal duct while inspecting the proximal duct. A choledochoscope is used to ensure the duct is clear both distally and proximally. Following this, the common bile duct is copiously irrigated to remove any remaining debris.

The surgeon instructs anesthesia to administer one milligram of glucagon to dilate the sphincter. Following this, the surgeon uses interrupted PDS sutures to close the incision in the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a large needle driver to identify the distal end of the choledochotomy and places a suture at the most distal end.

The surgeon completes the closure and confirms duct clearance with a cholangiogram. The common bile duct appears dilated to 1.5 cm. A clip remover is used to remove clips from the cystic duct without damaging tissue. The catheter is inserted into the cystic duct, and the final cholangiogram shows good flow to the proximal hepatic branches and distal common bile duct into the duodenum. The surgeon uses clips to secure the cystic duct.
